article:
Chunk # 0.
                         http://www.bio -protocol.org /e961                       Vol 3, Iss 21,  Nov 05, 2013 
 
Mitochondrial Isolation and Purification from Mouse Spinal Cord 
Philippe A Parone*, Sandrine Da Cruz, Don W Cleveland  
 
Ludwig Institute for Cancer Research and Department of Cellular and Molecular Medicine, 
University of California at San Diego (UCSD), La Jolla, CA, USA 
*For correspondence: philippeparone@gmail.com  
 
[Abstract ] Mitochondria are eukaryotic organelles that play a crucial role in several cellular 
processes, including energy production, β -oxidation of fatty acids and regulation of calcium 
homeostasis  In the last 20 years there has been a hightened interest in the st udy of mitochondria 
following the discoveries that mitochondria are central to the process of programmed cell death and that m itochondrial dysfunction s are implicated in numerous diseases including a wide range 
of neurological dis orders  such as Parkinson’ s disease , Alzheimer ’s disease, Huntington’ s disease 
and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis  In order to identify and study changes in mitochondrial function 
related to specific neurological conditions the mitochondria are often isolated from the 
compartment of the central nervous system most affected during disease  Here, we describe a 
protocol for the isolation of mitochondria from mouse spinal cord, a compartment of the central 
nervous system that is significantly affected in neuromuscular diseases such as amy otrophic 
lateral sclerosis This method relies on differential centrifugation to separate the mitochondria  
from the other subcellular compartments  
 
Materials and Reagents  
 1 Isoflurane (Isosol ) (Vedco , catalog number:  50201)  
2 Fatty acid (FA) free bovine serum albumin (Sigma -Aldrich , catalog number: 85041C)  
3 cOmplete
TM ULTRA protease inhibitor tablets, EDTA -free (Roche , catalog number: 
0589253001)  
4 OptiPrepTM density gradient medium ( Sigma -Aldrich , catalog number:  D1556)  
5 Bradford reagent (Bio- Rad, catalog number:  500-0001 ) 
6 Mannitol (Sigma -Aldrich, catalog number: M4125)  
7 Phosphate buffer saline (PBS) (see Recipes)  
8 1 M Tris -HCl (see Recipes)  
9 0.5 M EDTA (see Recipes)  
10 Buffer M  (see Recipe s) 
11 Buffer M + 0.45% FA free BSA + protease inhibitors  (see Recipes ) 
12 Buffer M + protease inhibitors  (see Recipes ) 
Copyright © 201 3 The Authors; exclusive licensee Bio- protocol LLC 1 
 Please cite this article as: Philippe et al., (2013) Mitochondrial Isolation and Purification from Mouse Spinal Cord, Bio-protocol 3 (21): e961 DOI:
10.21769/BioProtoc.961                          http://www.bio -protocol.org /e961                       Vol 3, Iss 21,  Nov 05, 2013 
Equipment  
 
1 10 ml disposable plastic syringe fitted (heat sealed) with a 200 μl pipette tip 
Using a Bunsen burner , heat and partially melt the wide end of a 200 μl pipette tip Before 
the plastic has time to harden quickly insert the melted end of the 200 μl pipette tip into 
the luer -lok of a disposable plastic 10 ml syringe  Let the assembly cool and check the 
tightness of the seal  by running 10 ml of water at high pressure through the syringe/tip  
assembly    
2 Sharp heavy di ssection duty scissors  
3 Euthanasia jar  
4 Balance and plastic weight boat  
5 Tweezers   
6 1 ml glass homogenizer with loose and tight pestles (Kimble Chase Kontes ) (Fisher 
Scientific , catalog number:  885300- 0001)  
7 Table top refrigerated centrifuge  
8 Ultrafuge  
9 Thinwall , Ultra -Clear™ 5  ml 13  x 51 mm ultrafuge tubes (Beckman Coulter , catalog 
number: 344057)  
10 SW55Ti swing- out rotor (Beckman Coulter , model : 342194)  
11 Pipettes  
12 50 ml conical  
 
Procedure  
 
A Preparation  
1 Put the following items on ice:  
a Homogenizer + pestles (those sho uld be placed in a 50 ml conical on ice)  
b 50 ml of Buffer M + 0.45% BSA + cOmpleteTM 
c 50 ml of Buffer M + cOmpleteTM 
2 Transfer the following items to the cold room:  
All the items placed on ice  
a Pipettes  
b Pipette tips  
c Tweezers  
d Small bucket of water to rinse pes tles 
e Swing out buckets for SW55Ti  
f      UltraClear SW55Ti tubes  
Copyright © 201 3 The Authors; exclusive licensee Bio- protocol LLC 2 
 Please cite this article as: Philippe et al., (2013) Mitochondrial Isolation and Purification from Mouse Spinal Cord, Bio-protocol 3 (21): e961 DOI:
10.21769/BioProtoc.961                          http://www.bio -protocol.org /e961                       Vol 3, Iss 21,  Nov 05, 2013 
g OptiPrep  
3 Set up the animal dissection area with:  
a Euthanasia chamber with Isoflurane  
b Heavy duty scissors 
c 10 ml syringe filled with PBS  
d Small plastic weigh trays placed on ice  
e Tweezers  
 
B The start  of procedure  
 
 
Figure  1 Procedure for purification of mitochondria from mouse spinal cord using 
differential centrifugation  
 
1 Terminally anesthetize the mouse using isoflurane in the euthanasia chamber  
2 Cervicaly dislocate the animal, decapitate and sec tion the spinal column at the iliac crest 
(just above the hips) Flush the spinal cord from the spinal column using the 10 ml 
syringe filled with PBS

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondrial Isolation and Purification from Mouse Spinal Cord**

**Procedure:**

A. Preparation
1. Put the following items on ice:
   a. Homogenizer + pestles (placed in a 50 ml conical on ice)
   b. 50 ml of Buffer M + 0.45% BSA + cOmpleteTM
   c. 50 ml of Buffer M + cOmpleteTM
2. Transfer the following items to the cold room:
   a. Pipettes
   b. Pipette tips
   c. Tweezers
   d. Small bucket of water to rinse pestles
   e. Swing out buckets for SW55Ti
   f. UltraClear SW55Ti tubes
   g. OptiPrep
3. Set up the animal dissection area with:
   a. Euthanasia chamber with Isoflurane
   b. Heavy duty scissors
   c. 10 ml syringe filled with PBS
   d. Small plastic weigh trays placed on ice
   e. Tweezers

B. The start of procedure
1. Terminally anesthetize the mouse using isoflurane in the euthanasia chamber.
2. Cervically dislocate the animal, decapitate, and section the spinal column at the iliac crest (just above the hips). Flush the spinal cord from the spinal column using the 10 ml syringe filled with PBS.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a protocol for the isolation of mitochondria from mouse spinal cord using differential centrifugation. The protocol includes the necessary materials and equipment, as well as the preparation steps and the starting procedure. However, there are missing recipes for Buffer M, Buffer M + 0.45% BSA + protease inhibitors, and Buffer M + protease inhibitors.

summary:
Summary (Iteration 4):
Mitochondrial isolation and purification from mouse spinal cord using differential centrifugation. Mitochondria, eukaryotic organelles, play a crucial role in cellular processes such as energy production, regulation of calcium homeostasis, and programmed cell death. Dysfunctions in mitochondria are implicated in various neurological diseases, including Parkinson's disease, Alzheimer's disease, Huntington's disease, and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. This method uses differential centrifugation to isolate mitochondria from other subcellular compartments in order to study changes in their function related to specific neurological conditions, such as amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. The isolated mitochondria can then be analyzed to understand the impact of mitochondrial dysfunction on diseases like Parkinson's disease.

article:
Chunk # 1.
Insert the tip at the caudal opening of the spinal column and eject 
about 5 ml of PBS This should flush out the spinal cord from the rostral end of the 
severed spinal column If a clean flushing of the spinal cord cannot be achieved, section 
the dorsal column below the front limbs and repeat the flushing procedure In this case 
make sure to save the piece of  spinal cord located within the small piece of spinal column 
resulting from the cut below the front -limbs Place the spinal cord, after removing excess 
PBS,  in the small plastic weigh trays placed on ice  
3 Weigh the spinal cord rapidly and place it back on ice 
4 Steps B5 to B18 are all performed in the cold room  
5 While working in the cold room, transfer the spinal cord with cold tweezers in the 1 ml 
glass homogenizer on ice Add 10 volumes of buffer M + 0.45% FA free BSA + 
cOmpleteTM (e.g if the spinal cord weighs 60 mg add 600 μl of buffer)  
6 Gently homogenize the tissue on ice with 10- 15 strokes (just enough to completely 
dissociate the tissue) using the cooled loose pestle Avoid forming air bubbles  
Note: Generating bubbles during the homogenization process can lead to disrupt ion of 
intracellular membranes and denaturing of proteins, both of which should be avoided to 
reduce damage to the mitochondria  
Copyright © 201 3 The Authors; exclusive licensee Bio- protocol LLC 3 
 Please cite this article as: Philippe et al., (2013) Mitochondrial Isolation and Purification from Mouse Spinal Cord, Bio-protocol 3 (21): e961 DOI:
10.21769/BioProtoc.961                          http://www.bio -protocol.org /e961                       Vol 3, Iss 21,  Nov 05, 2013 
7 Gently Homogenize the tissue on ice with 15 strokes using the tight pestle Avoid forming 
air bubbles  
8 Centrifuge at 1,000 x g for 5 min at 4 °C and transfer the supernatant  (S1) to a fresh tube 
on ice  
Note:The 1,000 x g supernatant generated at this step is composed mostly of cytosol with 
heavy and light membranes The pellet contains mostly unbroken cells and nuclei  
9 Resuspend the pellet  (P1) in 10 volumes of buffer M + 0.45% FA free BSA + cOmpleteTM 
and gently homogenize 15x using the tight pestle Avoid forming air bubbles  
Note:This step is used to further release cytosol and intracellular organelles from the 
remaining unbr oken cells  
10 Centrifuge at 1,000 x g for 5 min at 4 °C and pool the resulting supernatant  (S1) with th e 
supernatant (S2)  from the step 8  
11 Centrifuge the pooled supernatants at 1,000 x g for 5 min at 4 °C  
Note: This step ensures that any contaminating unbr oken cells or nuclei are removed 
from the cytosol/intracellular membrane subcellular fraction  
12 Collect supernatant (S3) staying clear of the pellet and spin it at 12,000 x g for 10 min at 
4 °C  
13 Prepare Beckman UltraClear™ tubes with 200 μl of OptiPrep™ and 600 μl of Buffer M + 
0.45% FA free BSA + cOmplete™ Mix well and store on ice  
14 Discard the supernatant  (S4) resulting from step B 12 and gently (but thoroughly) 
resuspend the pellet  (P4) using in 150 μl of buffer M + 0.45% FA free BSA + cOmpleteTM  
Note :The 12,000 x g supernatant generated at this step is composed mostly of cytosol 
with light membranes The pellet contains mostly heavy intracellular membranes 
(mitochondria)  
15 Once the pellet is completely resuspended transfer all of it (recording the final volume) 
into the Beckman UltraClearTM tubes prepared in step B13 Complement with buffer M + 
0.45% FA free BSA + cOmpleteTM to make a final volume of 1 ml and mix gently (but 
thoroughly) with a 1 ml pipette  
16 Transfer the UltraClearTM tubes to the SW55Ti swing out buckets on ice and centrifuge 
the tubes in a SW55Ti rotor using a high speed centrifuge at 17,000 x g (equivalent to 
13,400 rpm) at 4 °C for 17 min (remove breaks)  
Note: The heavy membrane fraction is centrifuged in an OptiPrep gradient to remove 
most of the contaminants (for example myelin and endoplasmic reticulum) from the heavy 
membrane preparation The UltraClearTM tubes can be reused if washed well after use 
(without detergent) and stored dry  
17 Gently remove the tubes from the buckets and place them on ice Using gentle aspiration 
remove s upernatant including the top white layer  
Copyright © 201 3 The Authors; exclusive licensee Bio- protocol LLC 4 
 Please cite this article as: Philippe et al., (2013) Mitochondrial Isolation and Purification from Mouse Spinal Cord, Bio-protocol 3 (21): e961 DOI:
10.21769/BioProtoc.961                          http://www.bio -protocol.org /e961                       Vol 3, Iss 21,  Nov 05, 2013 
18

mitochondria.protocol:
Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:

1. "Insert the tip at the caudal opening of the spinal column and eject about 5 ml of PBS. This should flush out the spinal cord from the rostral end of the severed spinal column. If a clean flushing of the spinal cord cannot be achieved, section the dorsal column below the front limbs and repeat the flushing procedure. In this case, make sure to save the piece of spinal cord located within the small piece of spinal column resulting from the cut below the front-limbs. Place the spinal cord, after removing excess PBS, in the small plastic weigh trays placed on ice."

2. "Weigh the spinal cord rapidly and place it back on ice."

3. "Steps B5 to B18 are all performed in the cold room."

4. "While working in the cold room, transfer the spinal cord with cold tweezers in the 1 ml glass homogenizer on ice. Add 10 volumes of buffer M + 0.45% FA free BSA + cOmpleteTM (e.g if the spinal cord weighs 60 mg add 600 μl of buffer)."

5. "Gently homogenize the tissue on ice with 10-15 strokes (just enough to completely dissociate the tissue) using the cooled loose pestle. Avoid forming air bubbles."

6. "Gently homogenize the tissue on ice with 15 strokes using the tight pestle. Avoid forming air bubbles."

7. "Centrifuge at 1,000 x g for 5 min at 4 °C and transfer the supernatant (S1) to a fresh tube on ice."

8. "Resuspend the pellet (P1) in 10 volumes of buffer M + 0.45% FA free BSA + cOmpleteTM and gently homogenize 15x using the tight pestle. Avoid forming air bubbles."

9. "Centrifuge at 1,000 x g for 5 min at 4 °C and pool the resulting supernatant (S1) with the supernatant (S2) from step 8."

10. "Centrifuge the pooled supernatants at 1,000 x g for 5 min at 4 °C."

11. "Collect supernatant (S3) staying clear of the pellet and spin it at 12,000 x g for 10 min at 4 °C."

12. "Prepare Beckman UltraClear™ tubes with 200 μl of OptiPrep™ and 600 μl of Buffer M + 0.45% FA free BSA + cOmplete™. Mix well and store on ice."

13. "Discard the supernatant (S4) resulting from step B12 and gently (but thoroughly) resuspend the pellet (P4) using 150 μl of buffer M + 0.45% FA free BSA + cOmpleteTM."

14. "Once the pellet is completely resuspended, transfer all of it (recording the final volume) into the Beckman UltraClearTM tubes prepared in step B13. Complement with buffer M + 0.45% FA free BSA + cOmpleteTM to make a final volume of 1 ml and mix gently (but thoroughly) with a 1 ml pipette."

15. "Transfer the UltraClearTM tubes to the SW55Ti swing out buckets on ice and centrifuge the tubes in a SW55Ti rotor using a high-speed centrifuge at 17,000 x g (equivalent to 13,400 rpm) at 4 °C for 17 min (remove breaks)."

16. "Gently remove the tubes from the buckets and place them on ice. Using gentle aspiration, remove supernatant including the top white layer."

Notes:
- Step 5 and Step 6 have

summary: Summary 4: Insert the tip at the caudal opening of the severed spinal column and flush with 5 ml of PBS to remove the spinal cord from the rostral end. If necessary, section the dorsal column below the front limbs and repeat the flushing procedure. Save the piece of spinal cord within the small piece of spinal column resulting from the cut. Place the spinal cord in a small plastic weigh tray on ice after removing excess PBS. Weigh the spinal cord rapidly using plastic weigh trays. Transfer the spinal cord to a 1 ml glass homogenizer on ice using cold tweezers. Gently homogenize the tissue on ice with 10-15 strokes to dissociate the tissue. Be cautious of generating bubbles during the homogenization process to avoid disrupting intracellular membranes and denaturing proteins. Centrifuge the homogenized tissue at 1g for 5 minutes at 4 °C and transfer the supernatant (S1) to a fresh tube on ice. Resuspend the resulting pellet (P1) in 10 volumes of buffer M + 0.45% FA free BSA + cOmpleteTM and centrifuge at 1g for 5 minutes at 4 °C. Combine the resulting supernatant (S1) with the supernatant (S2) from step 8 and centrifuge the pooled supernatants at 1g for 5 minutes at 4 °C to remove unbroken cells and nuclei. Collect the clear supernatant (S3) and spin it at 12g for 10 minutes at 4 °C. Prepare Beckman UltraClear™ tubes with OptiPrep™ and Buffer M + 0.45% FA free BSA + cOmplete™. Discard the resulting supernatant (S4) and gently resuspend the pellet (P4) in 150 μl of buffer M + 0.45% FA free BSA + cOmpleteTM. Transfer the completely resuspended pellet into the prepared Beckman UltraClearTM tubes. Complement with buffer M + 0.45% FA free BSA + cOmpleteTM to make a final volume of 1 ml and mix gently. Centrifuge the tubes in a SW55Ti rotor using a high-speed centrifuge at 17g for 17 minutes at 4 °C to remove contaminants. Carefully remove the tubes from the buckets and place them on ice. Use gentle aspiration to remove the supernatant, including the top white layer. Proceed with mitochondrial isolation and purification. (Citation: Bio-protocol 3 (21): e961, http://www.bio-protocol.org/e961)

topics.justification: The summary provides detailed instructions on how to extract and prepare the spinal cord for further analysis.
The summary mentions the process of homogenizing the tissue and emphasizes the need to be cautious to avoid disrupting intracellular membranes and denaturing proteins.
The summary describes the steps of centrifugation and collecting the supernatant at different stages of the procedure.
The summary concludes by mentioning that the next step is to proceed with mitochondrial isolation and purification.
The summary mentions the specific materials and buffers used in the procedure, such as PBS, buffer M, FA free BSA, and cOmpleteTM.

article:
Chunk # 2.
Gently resuspend the pellet  (P5) in 1 ml of buffer M + cOmplete™ (without completely 
dispersing it) and centrifuge at 12,000 x g for 10 min at 4 °C  
Note: After t he OptiPrep gradient the heavy membranes are resuspended in buffer M 
without BSA so as to wash off the BSA from the previous steps to more accurately 
measure the protein concentration in step B20 
19 Resuspend the pellet (P6) in 10- 20 μl of buffer M + cOmpleteTM by gently flicking the tube 
with your fingers and placing back on ice as quickly as possible Make sure pellet is 
completely resuspended before perform ing the protein quantification  
Note: It is important to resuspend the heavy membranes at a high concentration since this reduces damage to the organelles Resuspending the organelles by flicking also 
reduces organelle damage  
20 Estimate protein quantity using a Bradford assay on 1 μl of resuspended pellet Once the 
protein concentration is determined, add a final concentration of 0.45% FA free BSA to 
the suspension using a 4.5% FA free BSA stock to avoid diluting the preparation  
Note:  FA free BSA is added back to the heavy membrane preparation after protein 
quantification to allow for better preservation of mitochondrial activity by reducing 
uncoupling of the organelle Ideally mitochondrial activity (oxygen consumption) should 
be measured on a fraction of the preparation to determine the quality of the organelle and 
the damage that may have been sustained during preparation  
 
Recipe s 
 
1 PBS (1  L) 
Start with 800 ml of distilled water  
Add 8 g of NaCl  
Add 0.2 g of KCl  
Add 1.44 g of Na 2HPO 4 
Add 0.24 g of KH 2PO 4 
Adjust the pH to 7.4 with HCl  
Add distilled wat er to a total volume of 1 L 
2 1 M Tris-HCl, pH 7.4 (500 ml) 
Mix 60.57 g of Tris base with 400 ml of dH 2O 
When Tris base is dissolved pH to 7.4 using concentrated HCl  
Complete to 500 ml with dH 2O 
Store d at 4 °C 
3 0.5 M EDTA, pH 8.0 (500 ml) 
Mix 73.06 g of EDTA with 400 ml of dH 2O 
Copyright © 201 3 The Authors; exclusive licensee Bio- protocol LLC 5 
 Please cite this article as: Philippe et al., (2013) Mitochondrial Isolation and Purification from Mouse Spinal Cord, Bio-protocol 3 (21): e961 DOI:
10.21769/BioProtoc.961                          http://www.bio -protocol.org /e961                       Vol 3, Iss 21,  Nov 05, 2013 
Adjust pH to 8.0 using concentrated KOH  
Complete to 500 ml with dH 2O 
Store d at 4 °C 
4 Buffer M  (500 ml) 
Mix 19.1 g of mannitol  into 400 ml of  dH2O  
Add 11.9 g of sucrose  
Add 5 ml of 1  M Tris -HCl pH  7.4  
Add 1 ml of  0.5 M EDTA pH  8.0  
Complete to 500 ml with dH 2O 
Filter sterilize (0 .22 μm) 
Store d at 4 °C 
Note: Before the start of the isolation procedure prepare Recipes 5 and 6  
5 Buffer M + 0.45% FA free BSA + protease inhibitors  
50 ml Buffer M + 0.225  g FA free BSA + 1 tablet of cOmplet eTM protease inhibitors 
(store d at 4 °C) 
6 Buffer M + protease inhibitors   
50 ml Buffer  M + 1 tablet of cOmpleteTM protease inhibitors (stored  at 4 °C) 
 
Acknowledgments  
 
This protocol is adapted from Parone et al (2013) and Vande Velde et al (2008)  
 
References    
 
1 Parone, P A., Da Cruz, S., Han, J S., McAlonis -Downes, M., Vetto, A P., Lee, S K., 
Tseng, E and Cleveland, D W (2013) Enhancing mitochondrial calcium buffering 
capacity reduces aggregation of misfolded SOD1 and motor neuron cell de ath without 
extending survival in mouse models of inherited amyotrophic lateral sclerosis  J Neurosci  
33(11): 4657- 4671   
2 Vande Velde, C., Miller, T M., Cashman, N R and Cleveland, D W (2008) Selective 
association of misfolded ALS -linked mutant SOD1 with the cytoplasmic face of

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for mitochondrial isolation:

1. Gently resuspend the pellet (P5) in 1 ml of buffer M + cOmplete™ (without completely dispersing it).
2. Centrifuge at 12,000 x g for 10 min at 4 °C.
   - Note: After the OptiPrep gradient, the heavy membranes are resuspended in buffer M without BSA to wash off the BSA from the previous steps for accurate protein concentration measurement.
3. Resuspend the pellet (P6) in 10-20 μl of buffer M + cOmpleteTM by gently flicking the tube with your fingers and placing it back on ice quickly.
   - Note: Make sure the pellet is completely resuspended before performing protein quantification. Resuspending the organelles by flicking reduces organelle damage.
4. Estimate protein quantity using a Bradford assay on 1 μl of the resuspended pellet.
   - Note: After determining the protein concentration, add a final concentration of 0.45% FA free BSA to the suspension using a 4.5% FA free BSA stock to avoid diluting the preparation.
   - Note: FA free BSA is added back to the heavy membrane preparation after protein quantification to preserve mitochondrial activity by reducing uncoupling of the organelle. Ideally, mitochondrial activity should be measured on a fraction of the preparation to assess the quality and damage sustained during preparation.

Conclusion:
The steps for mitochondrial isolation are provided in the article. However, there are some protocol gaps and uncertainties. The article does not mention the initial steps of the isolation procedure, and it refers to previous steps involving OptiPrep gradient and BSA washing without providing details. Additionally, the article does not provide a complete recipe for buffer M, and it does not specify the protein quantification method used in the Bradford assay.

summary:
Revised Summary:
1. After the OptiPrep gradient, resuspend the heavy membranes (P5) in 1 ml of buffer M + cOmplete™ without completely dispersing it, then centrifuge at 12,000 x g for 10 min at 4 °C.
2. Wash off BSA from previous steps to accurately measure protein concentration.
3. Gently resuspend the pellet (P6) in 10-20 μl of buffer M + cOmplete™ by flicking the tube to reduce organelle damage.
4. Estimate protein quantity using a Bradford assay on 1 μl of the resuspended pellet.
5. Add a final concentration of 0.45% FA-free BSA to the suspension using a 4.5% FA-free BSA stock to avoid dilution.
6. Prepare 1 liter of PBS by adding NaCl, KCl, Na2HPO4, KH2PO4, and adjusting the pH to 7.4 with HCl.
7. Prepare 500 ml of buffer M by mixing mannitol, sucrose, 1 M Tris-HCl pH 7.4, and 0.5 M EDTA pH 8.0, then filter sterilize and store at 4 °C.
8. Prepare a stock of FA-free BSA in distilled water.
9. Measure mitochondrial activity (oxygen consumption) on a fraction of the preparation to assess organelle quality and potential damage.
10. This protocol is adapted from Parone et al (2013) and Vande Velde et al (2008).
clean_entities: Gently resuspend the pellet (P5) in 1 ml of buffer M + cOmplete™ (without completely dispersing it)
heavy membranes are resuspended in buffer M without BSA
wash off the BSA from the previous steps to more accurately measure the protein concentration
Resuspend the pellet (P6) in 10- 20 μl of buffer M + cOmpleteTM by gently flicking the tube with your fingers and placing back on ice as quickly as possible
Make sure pellet is completely resuspended before performing the protein quantification
It is important to resuspend the heavy membranes at a high concentration since this reduces damage to the organelles
Resuspending the organelles by flicking also reduces organelle damage
Estimate protein quantity using a Bradford assay on 1 μl of resuspended pellet
add a final concentration of 0.45% FA free BSA to the suspension using a 4.5% FA free BSA stock to avoid diluting the preparation
FA free BSA is added back to the heavy membrane preparation after protein quantification to allow for better preservation of mitochondrial activity by reducing uncoupling of the organelle

Ideally mitochondrial activity (oxygen consumption) should be measured on a fraction of the preparation to determine the quality of the organelle and the damage that may have been sustained during preparation
Buffer M + 0.45% FA free BSA + protease inhibitors
This protocol is adapted from Parone et al (2013) and Vande Velde et al (2008)

topics.justification: The summary mentions the use of OptiPrep gradient and centrifugation to separate heavy membranes.
The summary mentions the need to wash off BSA and measure protein concentration accurately.
The summary describes the gentle resuspension of the pellet to reduce organelle damage.
The summary mentions the use of a Bradford assay to estimate protein quantity.
The summary mentions the addition of FA-free BSA to the suspension to avoid dilution.
The summary describes the preparation of PBS using specific components and pH adjustment.
The summary describes the preparation of buffer M using specific components and sterilization.
The summary mentions the preparation of a stock of FA-free BSA in distilled water.
The summary mentions the measurement of mitochondrial activity to assess organelle quality.
The summary mentions that the protocol is adapted from specific research papers.

hypothetical_questions.question: What is the purpose of resuspending the heavy membranes in buffer M + cOmplete™ after the OptiPrep gradient?
How does washing off BSA from previous steps help in accurately measuring protein concentration?
Imagine you accidentally dispersed the heavy membranes completely while resuspending them in buffer M + cOmplete™. How would this affect the subsequent steps and the overall experiment?
How does the use of FA-free BSA in the suspension relate to the goal of avoiding dilution?
Have you ever encountered challenges in estimating protein quantity using the Bradford assay? How did you overcome them?
